items.q: What happens when you choose a wider aperture (smaller f-number)?
items.correct: More light enters and the depth of field becomes shallower.
items.misconception: A bigger f-number means a bigger aperture opening.
items.why: The inverse f-number scale trips students up: f/2.8 is a much larger opening than f/16.

items.q: Does raising the ISO simply make photos better in low light?
items.correct: It increases sensitivity but adds noise/grain, so it is a trade-off.
items.misconception: Higher ISO improves image quality in all conditions.
items.random_wrong: ISO controls the colour balance of the image.
items.why: Students treat ISO as a free brightness boost and miss the cost in noise and lost detail.

items.q: Is a slow shutter speed always a mistake?
items.correct: No — deliberate motion blur and light trails are creative uses of slow shutters.
items.misconception: Yes — sharp images are the only correct photographs.
items.random_wrong: No — shutter speed has no effect on blur.
items.why: Students equate technical sharpness with quality, overlooking intentional blur as an expressive choice.
The answer examiners want is: f-stops. “ISO” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Cartier-Bresson. “Ansel Adams” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 1842. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
